title: A shaft stopped dead against a terminal block. The `reject` keyword in Precept declares a path that exists in the definition but leads to a structural dead end rather than a transition. The shaft is visible — the path is real and named. But the terminal block is the definition's architectural wall: the path reaches it and stops permanently. Prevention is not a runtime guard — it is a structural endpoint written into the contract.
conceptualBridge: The reject wall means Precept's `reject` declaration is a structural terminal because the path exists in the definition but ends at a governed wall rather than a transition — the rejection is architectural, not a runtime check.
decision: Kept exploring: the shaft-and-wall form is directly language-derived and scores 4.31 avg; the vivid red palette is new to the active set — worth iterating on the silhouette to make it more distinct.
